task_background: Unlock the secrets of the cosmos with this comprehensive astronomical dataset, perfect for researchers, data scientists, and space enthusiasts. This meticulously curated collection offers key parameters for understanding stellar phenomena, making it an invaluable resource for machine learning projects, astrophysical research, and educational purposes.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
Temperature (K) : Dive into the thermal properties of stars, crucial for understanding their lifecycle and energy output.
Luminosity(L/Lo) : Explore stellar brightness relative to our Sun (Lo = 3.828 x 10^26 Watts), offering insights into star size and energy production.
Radius(R/Ro) : Compare star sizes to our Sun (Ro = 6.995 x 10^8 m), essential for grasping stellar evolution and structure.
Absolute magnitude(Mv) : Analyze intrinsic stellar brightness, standardized for comparative studies across vast cosmic distances.
Star type : Categorize celestial objects into six classes (0-Brown Dwarf, 1-Red Dwarf, 2-White Dwarf, 3-Main Sequence, 4-Supergiants, 5-Hypergiants), ideal for classification tasks.
Star color : Visualize the spectrum of stellar hues, providing clues about temperature and composition.
Spectral Class : Examine the visual signatures of stars, offering insights into their chemical makeup and evolutionary stage.
## This dataset is perfect for:
1. Machine learning and data science projects focused on star classification and prediction
2. Astrophysics research and celestial body analysis
3. Educational tools for astronomy and space science courses
4. Visualization projects exploring the diversity of stellar objects
Whether you're training models to classify stars, studying stellar evolution, or simply exploring the wonders of the universe, this dataset provides a solid foundation for your cosmic endeavors. Dive into the data and let the stars guide your discoveries!

task_background:
A CSV file with raw data for 157 masers, 2082 open clusters, 232 HII regions and 86 OB associations that appear on the maps.
Gaia is a space observatory of the European Space Agency, launched in 2013 and expected to operate until 2025. The spacecraft is designed for astrometry: measuring the positions, distances and motions of stars with unprecedented precision.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
type: The category of the astronomical object (e.g., maser, open cluster, hii region, ob association).
name: The name or designation of the object.
glon: Galactic longitude of the object, measured in degrees.
glat: Galactic latitude of the object, measured in degrees.
distance (parsecs): The distance of the object from the Sun, measured in parsecs.
x (parsecs): The object's position in the Galactic coordinate system along the X-axis, measured in parsecs.
y (parsecs): The object's position in the Galactic coordinate system along the Y-axis, measured in parsecs.
z (parsecs): The object's position in the Galactic coordinate system along the Z-axis, measured in parsecs.
radius (parsecs): The estimated radius of the object, measured in parsecs.
log10 age (years): The logarithm (base 10) of the object's estimated age, measured in years.
Arm (masers): The spiral arm of the Milky Way in which the maser is located (for maser sources only).
Source: The reference or dataset from which the object information was obtained.
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Gaia Astronomical Data.csv

task_background:
This dataset is curated by an Astronomy and Astrophysics researcher with a strong background in Mathematics and a keen interest in data science, machine learning, and deep learning. Machine learning is increasingly being utilized to address various challenges in Astronomy and Astrophysics, with asteroid-related studies serving as a compelling application for classification and regression tasks.
The data has been sourced from the Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) at the California Institute of Technology, an organization under NASA. It encompasses a comprehensive collection of asteroid-related information. The dataset includes various attributes relevant to asteroids, with basic definitions of the columns provided.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
id: Object internal database ID
spkid: Object primary SPK-ID
full_name: Object full name/designation
pdes: Object primary designation
name: Object IAU name
prefix: comet designation prefix
neo: Near-Earth Object (NEO) flag
pha: Potentially Hazardous Asteroid (PHA) flag
H: Absolute Magnitude, The visual magnitude denoted by H of an asteroid is measured from where observation was placed at unit heliocentric and geocentric distances at zero phase angle.
diameter: object diameter (from equivalent sphere) km Unit
albedo: Albedo is the ratio of the light received by a physical body to the light reflected by the body at zero phase angle with the same position and apparent size. Its value ranges from 0 to 1.
diameter_sigma: 1-sigma uncertainty in object diameter km Unit
orbit_id: Orbit solution ID
epoch: Epoch of osculation in modified Julian day form
epoch_mjd: epoch of osculation in modified Julian day form (TDB)
epoch_cal: epoch of osculation in calendar date/time form (TDB)
equinox: Equinox of reference frame
e: Eccentricity denoted by e is an orbital parameter that describes the structure of orbit. It is a ratio between foci to semi-major axis.
a: Semi-major axis of an elliptical orbit is half of the major axis. It is denoted by a.
q: Perihelion distance denoted by q is the closest distance between the orbiting body (Here, e.g., Asteroids) and the sun.
i: Inclination denoted by i is the angle between a vector normal to the orbiting body’s orbit plane and the ecliptic plane as the reference plane.
om: Longitude of the ascending node, The longitude of the ascending node denoted by om is the angle between the inertial frame vernal equinox and the point of passing up through the reference frame.
w: Argument of perihelion, Argument of perihelion denoted by w is the angle between the ascending node line and the perihelion point in the direction of orbit.
ma: Mean anomaly denoted by ma is the product of mean motion of orbiting body and past perihelion passage.
ad: Aphelion distance denoted by the ad is the farthest distance between the orbiting body (Here, e.g., Asteroids) and the sun.
n: Mean motion denoted by n is the angular speed at unit degree per day to complete one orbit around an ideal ellipse.
tp: Time of perihelion passage TDB Unit
tp_cal: time of perihelion passage (TDB)
per: sidereal orbital period (d)
per_y: sidereal orbital period (years)
moid: Earth Minimum Orbit Intersection Distance (au)
moid_ld: Earth Minimum Orbit Intersection Distance au Unit
sigma_e: eccentricity (1-sigma uncertainty)
sigma_a: semi-major axis (1-sigma uncertainty) (au)
sigma_q: perihelion distance (1-sigma uncertainty) (au)
sigma_i: inclination (1-sigma uncertainty) (deg)
sigma_om: long. of the asc. node (1-sigma uncertainty) (deg)
sigma_w: argument of perihelion (1-sigma uncertainty) (deg)
sigma_ma: mean anomaly (1-sigma uncertainty) (deg)
sigma_ad: aphelion distance (1-sigma uncertainty) (au)
sigma_n: mean motion (1-sigma uncertainty) (deg/d)
sigma_tp: time of peri. passage (1-sigma uncertainty) (d)
sigma_per: sidereal orbital period (1-sigma uncertainty) (d)
class: orbit classification
rms: normalized RMS of orbit fit (arcsec)
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Asteroid Dataset.csv

task_background: This dataset was collected by the Hipparcos satellite. The Tycho-2 catalog, derived from the same mission, contains a significantly larger collection of stars compared to the original Hipparcos dataset, albeit with lower measurement accuracy.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
TYC: Identifier (TYC number)
pflag: mean position flag
mRAdeg: [ deg ] Mean Right Ascension (ICRS, J2000)
mDEdeg: [ deg ] Mean Declination (ICRS, J2000)
pmRA: [ mas/yr ] Proper motion in RA*cos(dec)
pmDE: [ mas/yr ] Proper motion in Dec
e_mRA: [ mas ] standard error of RA*cos(dec) at mean epoch
e_mDE: [ mas ] standard error of Dec at mean epoch
e_pmRA: [ mas/yr ] standard error of proper motion in RA*cos(dec)
e_pmDE: [ mas/yr ] standard error of proper motion in Dec
mepRA: [ yr ] mean epoch of RA
mepDE: [ yr ] mean epoch of Dec
num: Number of positions used
g_mRA: Goodness of fit for mean RA
g_mDE: Goodness of fit for mean Dec
g_pmRA: Goodness of fit for pmRA
g_pmDE: Goodness of fit for pmDE
BTmag: [ mag ] Tycho-2 BT magnitude
e_BTmag: [ mag ] standard error of BT
VTmag: [ mag ] Tycho-2 VT magnitude
e_VTmag: [ mag ] standard error of VT
prox: [ 0.1arcsec ] proximity indicator
TYC1: Tycho-1 star flag
HIP: Hipparcos number
CCDM: CCDM component identifier for HIP stars
RAdeg: [ deg ] Observed Tycho-2 Right Ascension (ICRS)
DEdeg: [ deg ] Observed Tycho-2 Declination (ICRS)
epRA: [ yr ] epoch-1990 of RAdeg
epDE: [ yr ] epoch-1990 of DEdeg
e_RA: [ mas ] standard error RA*cos(dec) of observed Tycho-2 RA
e_DE: [ mas ] standard error of observed Tycho-2 Dec
posflg: type of Tycho-2 solution flag
corr: correlation (RAdeg,DEdeg)
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Tycho Astrometry Catalog.csv

task_background:
This dataset is a simulated collection designed for studying the expansion rate of the universe. It focuses on Type Ia Supernovae, which exhibit a nearly constant absolute magnitude at their peak brightness. Astronomers utilize this characteristic to determine the distances to supernovae and, consequently, the distances to their host galaxies. Once the galaxy's distance is established, spectral data are used to measure its redshift, which indicates the galaxy's velocity relative to us.
The primary objective of this dataset is to explore the relationship between a galaxy’s distance and its velocity. Due to the universe's expansion, more distant galaxies tend to move away from us at higher velocities, providing key insights into cosmological models and the Hubble constant.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
apparent_magnitude: Peak apparent magnitude values of type I-a supernovae
redshift: Redshift values of host galaxies containing the type I-a supernovae
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\simulated Hubbles Law Data.csv

data_name: Solar Flares from RHESSI Mission Data
task_background:
The dataset originates from the Reuven Ramaty High Energy Solar Spectroscopic Imager (RHESSI), a NASA solar flare observatory launched on February 5, 2002. RHESSI was part of the Small Explorer program (SMEX-6) and was initially named High Energy Solar Spectroscopic Imager (HESSI) before being renamed in honor of Reuven Ramaty on March 29, 2002. The mission's primary objective was to investigate particle acceleration and energy release in solar flares.
 
RHESSI was developed by Spectrum Astro for NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center and has been operated by the Space Sciences Laboratory in Berkeley, California. The principal investigator from 2002 to 2012 was Robert Lin, later succeeded by Säm Krucker.
 
Dataset Notes:
Only events with a non-zero position and an energy range different from 3-6 keV are confirmed as solar sources.
Events without position information that primarily appear in the front detectors but cannot be imaged are flagged as "PS".
Events lacking a valid position are considered non-solar only if the NS flag is set.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
flare: serial number
start.date: flare discovery date
start.time: flare start time
peak: peak counts/second in energy range 6-12 keV, averaged over active collimators, including background.
end: flare end time
duration.s: flare duration
peak.c/s: peak count rate in corrected counts.
total.counts: counts in energy range 6-12 keV integrated over duration of flare summed over all subcollimators, including background.
energy.kev: the highest energy band in which the flare was observed. Electron Kev (kilo electron volt)
x.pos.asec: the X-coordinate of the flare’s position in arcseconds.
y.pos.asec: the Y-coordinate of the flare’s position in arcseconds.
radial: the offset of the flare position from the spin axis of the spacecraft in arcsec. This is used ispectroscopy.
active.region.ar: A number for the closest active region, if available

The flag.1 - flag.5 fields represent flare flag codes, where each row of data may contain multiple flag codes. The specific flag codes and their corresponding descriptions are as follows:
A0 - Attenuator state 0 (None) sometime during the flare.
A1 - Attenuator state 1 (Thin) sometime during the flare.
A2 - Attenuator state 2 (Thick) sometime during the flare.
A3 - Attenuator state 3 (Both) sometime during the flare.
An - Attenuator state at the peak of the flare (0=None, 1=Thin, 2=Thick, 3=Both).
DF - Front segment counts were decimated sometime during the flare.
DR - Rear segment counts were decimated sometime during the flare.
GD - Data gap occurred during the flare.
GE - Flare ended in a data gap.
GS - Flare started in a data gap.
ED - The spacecraft was in eclipse (night) sometime during the flare.
EE - The flare ended while the spacecraft was in eclipse.
ES - The flare started while the spacecraft was in eclipse.
FE - Flare was ongoing at the end of the file.
FS - Flare was ongoing at the start of the file.
FR - The spacecraft was in Fast Rate Mode during the flare.
MR - The spacecraft was in a high-latitude zone during the flare.
SD - The spacecraft was in the South Atlantic Anomaly (SAA) sometime during the flare.
SE - The flare ended while the spacecraft was in the SAA.
SS - The flare started while the spacecraft was in the SAA.
NS - Non-solar event.
PE - Particle event: Particles were present.
PS - Possible solar flare detected in front detectors, but no position was determined.
Pn - Position Quality (P0 = Position is NOT valid, P1 = Position is valid).
Qn - Data Quality (Q0 = Highest Quality, Q11 = Lowest Quality).
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Solar Flares from RHESSI Mission Data.csv

task_background:
The dataset comprises 100,000 observations extracted from the Data Release (DR) of a digital celestial survey. It is designed for the "Celestial Phenomena Catalog Challenge," where participants are required to develop machine learning models to classify celestial objects into one of four categories: STAR, GALAXY, Quasi-Stellar Object (QSO), or Quasar. Each observation in the dataset is characterized by 42 features, which include identifiers, coordinates, photometric data, and other attributes commonly used by astronomers to analyze celestial objects. The challenge involves leveraging these features to build accurate classification models.
 
When evaluating the models, it is crucial to select an appropriate metric that aligns with the competition's objectives and addresses the specific challenges of the dataset. Considerations such as class imbalance, the significance of different error types, and the practical implications of the classification task should guide the choice of evaluation criteria.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
"ObjectID" and "SpectrographObjectID" - Unique identifiers for celestial objects.
"RightAscension" - J2000 Right Ascension of the celestial object.
"Declination" - J2000 Declination of the celestial object.
"FinalRedshift" - Final Redshift of the celestial object.
"UltravioletMagnitude," "GreenMagnitude," "RedMagnitude," "InfraredMagnitude," and "NearInfraredMagnitude" - Best DeV/Exp magnitude fits in the respective photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).
"RunNumber" - Run number indicating the specific period of SDSS observation.
"DataRerunNumber" - Rerun number for data reprocessing.
"CameraColumnNumber" - Camera column number responsible for imaging the observation.
"FieldNumber" - Field number specifying the portion of the sky imaged during the observation.
"PlateNumber" - Plate number representing the glass plate on the telescope.
"OpticalFiberID" - Optical Fiber ID responsible for gathering light from the celestial object.
"ModifiedJulianDate" - Modified Julian Date representing the time of observation.
"PetrosianRadiusU," "PetrosianRadiusG," "PetrosianRadiusR," "PetrosianRadiusI," and "PetrosianRadiusZ" - Petrosian radii for the five photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).
"PetrosianFluxU," "PetrosianFluxG," "PetrosianFluxR," "PetrosianFluxI," and "PetrosianFluxZ" - Petrosian fluxes for the five photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).
"PetrosianHalfLightRadiusU," "PetrosianHalfLightRadiusG," "PetrosianHalfLightRadiusR," "PetrosianHalfLightRadiusI," and "PetrosianHalfLightRadiusZ" - Petrosian half-light radii for the five photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).
"PSFMagnitudeU," "PSFMagnitudeG," "PSFMagnitudeR," "PSFMagnitudeI," and "PSFMagnitudeZ" - Magnitudes measured using the Point Spread Function (PSF) for the five photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).
"ExponentialAxisRatioU," "ExponentialAxisRatioG," "ExponentialAxisRatioR," "ExponentialAxisRatioI," and "ExponentialAxisRatioZ" - Axis ratios of exponential fits to the light profile of celestial objects in the five photometric bands (u, g, r, i, and z).

## Note:
These parameters collectively aid in characterizing the properties of celestial objects, facilitating the study of their morphologies, sizes, and evolutionary trends. The dataset provides a comprehensive view of astronomical objects, including stars, galaxies, and quasars, in different photometric bands, enabling diverse research and analysis within the field of astronomy.

task_background:
The dataset captures the variations in flux (light intensity) of several thousand stars, each labeled with a binary classification of 1 or 2. A label of 2 signifies that the star has at least one confirmed exoplanet in orbit, with some being multi-planet systems.
 
Since planets do not emit light, their presence is inferred from periodic dimming in a star's flux over extended observation periods, typically months or years. Such dimming events suggest the possibility of an orbiting body, classifying the star as a ‘candidate’ system. Further observations, particularly at different wavelengths using specialized satellites, can provide additional evidence to confirm the presence of an exoplanet.
 
##Potential Future Strategies
Data Augmentation: Given that the observations form time series data, techniques such as systematic shifting and noise injection could be used to create additional synthetic but realistic time series.
Deep Autoencoders & Clustering: Unsupervised learning approaches, such as clustering via deep autoencoders, could reveal new insights and patterns in the data.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
Column 1: Binary label (1: non-exoplanet, 2: confirmed exoplanet)
Columns 2-3,198（e.g., FLUX.1,FLUX.2,...）: Flux values recorded over time
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ts_data\Kepler labelled time series data.csv

data_name: Star dataset to predict star types
task_background: This is a dataset consisting of several features of stars. The purpose of making the dataset is to prove that the stars follows a certain graph in the celestial Space , specifically called Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram or simply HR-Diagram so that we can classify stars by plotting its features based on that graph.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
Temperature (K) - This column consists of the Surface temperatures of several stars.
Luminosity(L/Lo) - This column consists of the Luminosity of several stars calculated with respect to sun(L/Lo).
Radius(R/Ro) - This column consists of the Radius of several stars calculated with respect to sun(R/Ro).
Absolute magnitude(Mv) - This column consists of the Absolute Visual magnitude(Mv) of several stars.
Star color - This column contains the info about the colors of each star after Spectral Analysis(white,Red,Blue,Yellow,yellow-orange etc).
Spectral Class - This column contains info about the spectral classes of each star(O,B,A,F,G,K,,M).
Star type - This column is the output class (6 classes ranging from 0-5) 0: Brown Dwarf, 1: Red Dwarf, 2: White Dwarf, 3: Main Sequence, 4: Supergiant, 5: Hypergiant.

## Note:
The Luminosity and radius of each star is calculated w.r.t. that of the values of Sun.
Lo = 3.828 x 10^26 Watts (Avg Luminosity of Sun)
Ro = 6.9551 x 10^8 m (Avg Radius of Sun)
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Star dataset to predict star types.csv

task_background: In astronomy, stellar classification is the classification of stars based on their spectral characteristics. The classification scheme of galaxies, quasars, and stars is one of the most fundamental in astronomy. The early cataloguing of stars and their distribution in the sky has led to the understanding that they make up our own galaxy and, following the distinction that Andromeda was a separate galaxy to our own, numerous galaxies began to be surveyed as more powerful telescopes were built. This datasat aims to classificate stars, galaxies, and quasars based on their spectral characteristics.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
The data consists of 100,000 observations of space taken by the SDSS (Sloan Digital Sky Survey). Every observation is described by 17 feature columns and 1 class column which identifies it to be either a star, galaxy or quasar.

obj_ID = Object Identifier, the unique value that identifies the object in the image catalog used by the CAS
alpha = Right Ascension angle (at J2000 epoch)
delta = Declination angle (at J2000 epoch)
u = Ultraviolet filter in the photometric system
g = Green filter in the photometric system
r = Red filter in the photometric system
i = Near Infrared filter in the photometric system
z = Infrared filter in the photometric system
run_ID = Run Number used to identify the specific scan
rereun_ID = Rerun Number to specify how the image was processed
cam_col = Camera column to identify the scanline within the run
field_ID = Field number to identify each field
spec_obj_ID = Unique ID used for optical spectroscopic objects (this means that 2 different observations with the same spec_obj_ID must share the output class)
class = object class (galaxy, star or quasar object)
redshift = redshift value based on the increase in wavelength
plate = plate ID, identifies each plate in SDSS
MJD = Modified Julian Date, used to indicate when a given piece of SDSS data was taken
fiber_ID = fiber ID that identifies the fiber that pointed the light at the focal plane in each observation
load_path: C:\Users\10412\Desktop\多模态大语言模型\Code\天文Code\AutoAstro\data\ml_data\Stellar Classification Dataset - SDSS17.csv

task_background: Traditional classification tasks
task_describe: The dataset comprises image data designed for solar spectrum classification, structured into training and test sets. Each image has a resolution of 120×120 pixels and belongs to one of three classes indexed as follows:0:'burst',1:'calibration',2:'non_burst'.The sample distribution is as follows:Training set—burst:925,calibration:790,non_burst:5,336;Test set—burst:232,calibration:198,non_burst:1,334.This dataset supports the training and evaluation of classification models for solar spectrum analysis.Data sources:https://github.com/filterbank/spectrumcls?tab=readme-ov-file

task_background: Traditional classification tasks

Each image has a resolution of 300×300 pixels and belongs to one of four classes indexed as follows:0:'Bent',1:'Compact',2:'FRI',3:'FRII'.The sample distribution is as follows:Training set—Bent:248,Compact:291,FRI:5,395,FRII:824;Test set—Bent:100,Compact:100,FRI:100,FRII:100.This dataset supports the training and evaluation of classification models for morphological classification of radio galaxies analysis.Data sources:https://zenodo.org/records/7692494

task_background: Traditional classification tasks

task_background: Multimodal classification task
task_describe: The dataset consists of multimodal image data specifically designed for the classification of transient detections, aimed at distinguishing between real astrophysical signals and bogus imaging artifacts captured by the Pan-STARRS telescope. Data structured into training and test sets (The annotation file is in JSON format.). Note: data consists of three three views: New, Reference and Difference. Each image has a resolution of 640×480 pixels and belongs to one of two classes indexed as follows: Real and Bogus. The sample distribution is as follows:Training set—Real:792, Bogus:806; Test set—Real:207, Bogus:193. This dataset supports the training and evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models for the classification of transient detections. Data sources: https://zenodo.org/records/14561778

task_background: This dataset is a bright star dataset.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
HIP：The Hipparcos Catalog is a high-precision stellar catalog created by the European Space Agency's (ESA) Hipparcos satellite. Each star is assigned a unique HIP identifier (e.g., HIP 12345).
RA：Stands for Right Ascension. Measured in degrees(0° to 360°). This is the celestial equivalent of longitude, specifying a star's position along the celestial equator.
Dec：Stands for Declination. Measured in degrees(-90° to 90°). This is the celestial equivalent of latitude, specifying a star's position north or south of the celestial equator.
vmag：Stands for Visual Magnitude (V-band magnitude).

task_background: The Photometric LSST Astronomical Time-Series Classification Challenge (PLAsTiCC) asks  to help prepare to classify the data from this new survey. This data can classify astronomical sources that vary with time into different classes.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
object_id: unique object identifier.
ra: right ascension, sky coordinate: co-longitude in degrees.
decl: declination, sky coordinate: co-latitude in degrees.
ddf: A flag to identify the object as coming from the DDF survey area (with value DDF = TRUE for the DDF, DDF = FALSE for the WFD survey). Note that while the DDF fields are contained within the full WFD survey area, the DDF fluxes have significantly smaller uncertainties.
host_specz: the spectroscopic redshift of the source. This is an extremely accurate measure of redshift, available for the training set and a small fraction of the test set.
host_photoz: The photometric redshift of the host galaxy of the astronomical source. While this is meant to be a proxy for host_specz, there can be large differences between the two and should be regarded as a far less accurate version of host_specz.
host_photoz_error: The uncertainty on the host_photoz based on LSST survey projections.
mwebv: MW E(B-V). this ‘extinction’ of light is a property of the Milky Way (MW) dust along the line of sight to the astronomical source, and is thus a function of
the sky coordinates of the source ra, decl. This is used to determine a passband dependent dimming and redenning of light from astronomical sources.
target: The class of the astronomical source.
The flux_0 - flux_27（e.g., fulx_0,fulx_1,...） fields represent measured flux (brightness) in the passband of observation.

task_background: The data originates from the variable star light curves generated by the Kepler. The first column represents the category labels, while the remaining columns consist of time-series photometric data [light curves] sampled at intervals of 0.02 days, ranging from 0 to 10 days.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
Label: The first column represents the class label of the light curve, which includes the categories ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F'].

The flux0 - flux500（e.g., flux_0,flux_1,...） fields represent measured flux (brightness) in the passband of observation.

task_background: The data is sourced from Loamst DR11, with a volume of approximately 100,000 entries, and can be used for time sequence prediction or classification tasks.
task_describe:
## The field description in the CSV file:
Type: unique object identifier.
Teff: Effective temperature (in Kelvin), representing the star's surface brightness.
logg: Surface gravity (logarithmic, in cgs units), indicating pressure/density (higher = compact stars like dwarfs).
[Fe/H]: Metallicity (log ratio relative to Sun), showing iron abundance (>0 = metal-rich, <0 = metal-poor).

The flux0 - flux4900（e.g., flux0,flux1,...） fields represent measured flux (brightness) in the passband of observation.
